subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 13, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Incretins Predict Response to Enteral Nutrition Strategies in
sentences: Story 1 is about Incretins Predict Response to Enteral Nutrition Strategies in the EDEN Trial: A Secondary Analysis..
This is a recent research article from American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
889 participants were included (452 randomized to trophic and 437 to full enteral nutrition).

GIP predicted HTE to enteral nutrition strategies (adjusted interaction p-value 0.01) with lower mortality from trophic feeds (14.1% vs 27.2% in full) in patients in the highest GIP tertile but similar mortality in other tertiles.
GLP-1, ARDS subphenotypes, and baseline risk of mortality did not predict HTE.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Nutrition and Exercise in Critical Illness (NEXIS) trial: rand
sentences: Story 2 is about Nutrition and Exercise in Critical Illness (NEXIS) trial: randomized trial of combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid plus usual care..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients 1:1 to protocolized in-bed cycling (45 min/d) plus intravenous amino acid (total protein intake: 2, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
In total, 115 patients (median age ∼55 years, 55% male, Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation II ∼24, >90% mechanically ventilated) were randomized (60 intervention; 55 control).
The median (interquartile range) 6MWD was similar between intervention and control: 108 (15-242) and 95 (0-196) m (P = .51) (median difference, 10; 95% CI, -75 to 84).
Enrollment was stopped early due to slow recruitment, with no evidence of safety concerns.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: May influence protocols or care pathways if the effect is reproducible; verify whether the outcome is patient-centered or mainly operational.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Neonatal Outcomes Following Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhib
sentences: Story 3 is about Neonatal Outcomes Following Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor Use During Pregnancy..
This is a recent research article from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Participants were women with singleton pregnancies (2006 to 2022) with SSRI use within the 2 years before conception and at least 1 prenatal visit during the first trimester (periconception tr, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

SSRI continuation was associated with lower Apgar scores at 1 minute (mean difference [MD], -0.39; 95% CI, -0.60 to -0.18) and 5 minutes (MD, -0.28; 95% CI, -0.42 to -0.13), and increased risk of meconium in amniotic fluid (odds ratio [OR], 1.73; 95% CI, 1.22 to 2.45).
No significant associations were found for congenital anomalies (OR, 1.09; 95% CI, 0.63 to 1.90), though this result should be interpreted with caution given limited sample size.
No differences were found for neonatal intensive care unit admission (OR, 1.23; 95% CI, 0.82 to 1.83) or other outcomes.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Incretins Predict Response to Enteral Nutrition Strategies in the EDEN Trial: A Secondary Analysis.
articles.source: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
articles.summary: 889 participants were included (452 randomized to trophic and 437 to full enteral nutrition). GIP predicted HTE to enteral nutrition strategies (adjusted interaction p-value 0.01) with lower mortality from trophic feeds (14.1% vs 27.2% in full) in patients in the highest GIP tertile but similar mortality in other tertiles. The Early versus Delayed Enteral Nutrition (EDEN) trial found no significant difference in mortality between trophic and full enteral nutrition strategies in acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) patients. Heterogeneity of treatment effect (HTE) has been identified in prior ARDS trials. We previously identified intestine-derived incretin hormones (glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide [GIP] and glucagon-like peptide [GLP]-1 as potential predictive biomarkers in the response to nutrition.
articles.analysis: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.

GLP-1, ARDS subphenotypes, and...

keyResults: 889 participants were included (452 randomized to trophic and 437 to full enteral nutrition).

GIP predicted HTE to enteral nutrition strategies (adjusted interaction p-value 0.01) with lower mortality from trophic feeds (14.1% vs 27.2% in full) in patients in the highest GIP tertile but similar mortality in other tertiles.
GLP-1, ARDS subphenotypes, and baseline risk of mortality did not predict HTE.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
articles.practiceImpact: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.

articles.title: Nutrition and Exercise in Critical Illness (NEXIS) trial: randomized trial of combined in-bed cycling and intravenous amino acid plus usual care.
articles.source: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
articles.summary: In total, 115 patients (median age ∼55 years, 55% male, Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation II ∼24, >90% mechanically ventilated) were randomized (60 intervention; 55 control). The median (interquartile range) 6MWD was similar between intervention and control: 108 (15-242) and 95 (0-196) m (P = .51) (median difference, 10; 95% CI, -75 to 84). Among patients with ARF, a combined intervention of early in-bed cycling and amino acid supplementation appeared safe but did not improve 6MWD or secondary outcomes during hospitalization or 6-month follow-up. Patients with acute respiratory failure (ARF) in intensive care units (ICUs) often experience immobility and limited nutritional intake, with long-lasting impairments. Evaluate whether combined in-bed cycling exercise and intravenous amino acid, initiated early in the ICU, improves physical functioning.
articles.analysis: May influence protocols or care pathways if the effect is reproducible; verify whether the outcome is patient-centered or mainly operational.

keyResults: In total, 115 patients (median age ∼55 years, 55% male, Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation II ∼24, >90% mechanically ventilated) were randomized (60 intervention; 55 control).
The median (interquartile range) 6MWD was similar between intervention and control: 108 (15-242) and 95 (0-196) m (P = .51) (median difference, 10; 95% CI, -75 to 84).
Enrollment was stopped early due to slow recruitment, with no evidence of safety concerns.
The mean duration of cycling was 40 ± 7 min/d (control: 0 min/d), with total protein intake of 1.9 ± 0.6 g/kg/d (control: 0.9 ± 0.6).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.

articles.title: Neonatal Outcomes Following Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor Use During Pregnancy.
articles.summary: The periconception trial examined congenital anomalies with first-trimester exposure, and the early-pregnancy trial evaluated nonanomaly outcomes with first- or second-trimester exposure. Participants were women with singleton pregnancies (2006 to 2022) with SSRI use within the 2 years before conception and at least 1 prenatal visit during the first trimester (periconception trial) or during the first or second trimester (early-pregnancy trial). SSRI continuation was associated with lower Apgar scores at 1 minute (mean difference [MD], -0.39; 95% CI, -0.60 to -0.18) and 5 minutes (MD, -0.28; 95% CI, -0.42 to -0.13), and increased risk of meconium in amniotic fluid (odds ratio [OR], 1.73; 95% CI, 1.22 to 2.45). No significant associations were found for congenital anomalies (OR, 1.09; 95% CI, 0.63 to 1.90), though this result should be interpreted with caution given limited sample size. No differences were found for neonatal intensive care unit admission (OR, 1.23; 95% CI, 0.82 to 1.83) or other outcomes.
articles.analysis: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.

pico.population: Participants were women with singleton pregnancies (2006 to 2022) with SSRI use within the 2 years before conception and at least 1 prenatal visit during the first trimester (periconception tr
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Apgar scores, meconium in amniotic fluid, neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) admission, preterm birth, birth weight, respiratory distress, feeding problems, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, pulmonary hypertension, cesarean delivery, and congenital anomalies.

keyResults: SSRI continuation was associated with lower Apgar scores at 1 minute (mean difference [MD], -0.39; 95% CI, -0.60 to -0.18) and 5 minutes (MD, -0.28; 95% CI, -0.42 to -0.13), and increased risk of meconium in amniotic fluid (odds ratio [OR], 1.73; 95% CI, 1.22 to 2.45).
No significant associations were found for congenital anomalies (OR, 1.09; 95% CI, 0.63 to 1.90), though this result should be interpreted with caution given limited sample size.
No differences were found for neonatal intensive care unit admission (OR, 1.23; 95% CI, 0.82 to 1.83) or other outcomes.
The early-pregnancy trial included 1014 pregnancies (mean [SD] maternal age, 30.3 [5.4] years).
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: For most ICU patients, this supports avoiding very tight glucose targets unless a protocol can reliably prevent severe hypoglycemia.
